advantages.compositional semantics: Dependent types in Lean 4 provide native, machine-checked functorial composition (e.g., via Pi-types and structure induction) superior to ad-hoc hierarchies in DDL/Catala or XML modularity in LegalRuleML, ensuring compositional correctness with FormalisationBoundary for legally vague boundaries.
advantages.deontic modalities: Dependent types provide machine-checked proofs that deontic conflicts are resolved (no obligation ∧ prohibition), parameterize modalities over contexts (Obligation ctx p), and use FormalisationBoundary to make interpretive discretion explicit and modular via typeclasses—superior to ad-hoc superiority relations or XML attributes.
advantages.dependent types (evidence-as-inhabitants: Lean 4's dependent types enable precise encoding of 'evidence inhabits legal propositions' via Curry-Howard, supporting case-specific refinements (e.g., {proof : is_contract_valid doc} → obligation_holds) and FormalisationBoundary theorems that compose machine-checked cores with interpretive discretion.
advantages.machine-checkable guarantees: Dependent types provide native encoding of legal relationships (e.g., 'obligation implies duty'), static guarantees absent in other systems, and FormalisationBoundary precisely delineates machine-checkable core from interpretive discretion—enabling compositional proofs across legal domains.
advantages.open texture encoding: Dependent types make Hart's core/penumbra distinction *type-native*: core cases typecheck automatically, penumbra requires explicit proofs (encoding judicial discretion), and FormalisationBoundary prevents invalid sorites inferences while preserving undecidability where required.
advantages.provenance tracking: Dependent types provide **type-level provenance** where rule applications and evidence dependencies are statically verified and preserved in proof terms, enabling complete audit trails that survive code evolution. FormalisationBoundary creates explicit **interpretation scopes** linking machine-checked proofs to natural language sources with precise boundary annotations.
advantages.temporal reasoning: Dependent types provide machine-checkable guarantees on temporal consistency (e.g., no rule applies outside effective dates), FormalisationBoundary rigorously encodes vagueness/interpretive discretion in expiry/retroactivity without losing decidability, surpassing defeasible logics in proof strength.

limitations.compositional semantics: Heavy reliance on manual theorem-proving for open-textured composition introduces scalability gaps versus automated default logics in DDL/Catala; lacks built-in priority mechanisms requiring explicit encoding.
limitations.deontic modalities: Lean proofs don't auto-generate from text (needs LLM assistance like DDL pipelines); typeclass diamonds/ambiguity require manual instance design; less human-readable than rule-based DDL for non-experts.
limitations.dependent types (evidence-as-inhabitants: Full dependent type formalisation scales poorly to complete statutes; FormalisationBoundary decisions remain human judgments requiring legal expertise, creating trusted oracles between formal and interpretive layers.
limitations.machine-checkable guarantees: Typechecking undecidable beyond simple fragments; requires human expertise to formalize boundaries correctly; currently lacks comprehensive legal libraries compared to Catala's tax domain coverage.
limitations.open texture encoding: Proof engineering overhead for complex penumbra cases; no built-in oracles for empirical vagueness ('reasonable time' requires external data integration); Lean 4 elaboration can obscure interpretive choices from non-expert stakeholders.
limitations.provenance tracking: Lean 4 proof terms remain opaque to non-experts despite rich provenance; human-readable explanation requires separate elaboration or visualization layers. Dependent type complexity hinders rapid prototyping compared to rule-based systems.
limitations.temporal reasoning: Requires explicit finite bounding of timelines for termination checking; continuous/vague time (e.g., 'soon after') approximated, losing full real-number expressiveness; heavy reliance on human formalization of open-textured boundaries.
